content_markdown:
The diagram below represents equipotential lines of a gravitational field.

Which of the following is the direction and strength of the field at point P?

diagram_metadata.description: A grid showing three curved equipotential lines labeled -10 J/kg, -20 J/kg, and -30 J/kg. A scale bar indicates 2 meters. Point P is located on the -20 J/kg line.

vibe_explanation: To find the field strength $g$, we use the relationship $g = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$. Looking at the grid, the distance between the $-10\text{ Jkg}^{-1}$ and $-30\text{ Jkg}^{-1}$ lines is 4 units on the grid. Since 2 units equals $2\text{ m}$, the distance $\Delta r$ is $4\text{ m}$. The change in potential $\Delta V$ is $20\text{ Jkg}^{-1}$. Thus, $g = \frac{20}{4} = 5.0\text{ Nkg}^{-1}$. The direction of the gravitational field is always from higher potential to lower potential (towards the more negative values), which in this diagram is to the right ($\rightarrow$).
